items.q: What are the 4 Cs of food hygiene?
items.correct: Cleaning, Cooking, Chilling, and avoiding Cross-contamination.
items.why: Students guess at the Cs. They specifically address the four food-safety risks: dirty surfaces, undercooked food, warm storage, and pathogens transferring between foods (raw to ready-to-eat).

items.q: On a traffic-light label, what does a red light indicate?
items.correct: High amounts of that nutrient per 100g - eat less often.
items.misconception: The food is dangerous and should never be eaten.
items.why: Students treat red as 'forbidden'. Traffic lights are a guidance system - red = high, eat less often or in small portions. Many foods (cheese, olive oil) are red on certain nutrients but fine in moderation.

items.q: Which macronutrient provides the most energy per gram?
items.why: Students assume carbs (the 'energy food') provide most energy per gram. Carbs and protein = 4 kcal/g; FAT = 9 kcal/g (most energy-dense). Vitamins provide no energy at all.
Correct answer: Gluten. “Caramel” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Liquid weeps from a set gel
items.why: Correct answer: Liquid weeps from a set gel. “Sugar caramelises” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Number of main wheat proteins that combine to form gluten?
items.why: Exact answer: 2. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
